body:
Tyler,

You asked about the morning schedule last week. Like I said—up at 5:45, coffee at the kitchen counter while Mom calls. She's better than any alarm clock. That hour before Karen wakes up is when I lock down the film work and answer shop emails. Keeps everything from sliding sideways.

Game weeks are different though. Some mornings I'm fried by the time practice starts. You mentioned an app or system a while back, and I pushed back, but I'm rethinking it. If it's simple—not a bunch of logging every five minutes—I could use something to keep the morning solid.

What did you have in mind?

Coach

Coach,

Glad you're open to it. I was thinking something low-key: a simple checklist on your phone or even a card by the coffee maker. Morning prep, film work, shop emails—just the core stuff. Nothing that takes time away from doing the actual work.

For game weeks especially, having the routine locked in is one less thing bouncing around your head when you're tired. That's when it matters most.

I can put something together this weekend. Let's touch base Friday.

Tyler

Hey Tyler,

Dorothy called this morning like she does—caught me between drills. She's worried about Dad again. Not anything specific, just that he's been quieter than usual, forgetting small things. You know how she gets. She wants to make sure we're all keeping an eye on things.

I told her I'd check in with him this week, maybe stop by after we finish up Friday. Figured you should know it might be a tighter week for me schedule-wise if I'm driving out there.

Thanks for keeping the shop running smooth while I'm stretched thin.

Henry

Coach,

Got it. No problem on the schedule—I can keep things covered at the shop. We've got the Saturday cuts locked in pretty solid anyway.

For your dad, that sounds like the kind of thing that needs checking in on. Don't worry about us. Focus on what matters.

If you need me to handle anything specific while you're out there—rescheduling appointments, covering extra hours—just say the word. You've always had my back.

Talk soon.

Tyler

Tyler,

Appreciate that. Means a lot coming from you.

I'll swing by Dad's Friday evening, just make sure he's doing okay and Mom's not carrying it all herself. Probably nothing, but you know how it is—family's family.

See you Saturday morning. Let's make it a good week at the shop.

Henry

Hey Tyler,

Quick question on the prep side—when you get a minute this week, can you help me think through the schedule for next week's practice drills? We're running a new coverage package and I want to make sure the breakdown makes sense before we hit the field. Also need to coordinate with Karen on dinner timing since we've got the barber shop doing inventory Thursday evening.

You free for a call Thursday after practice?

Thanks,
Dad

Dad,

Absolutely, I can make Thursday work. Why don't we hop on around 5:30 after you finish up at the shop? I can pull together some notes on the coverage angles and we can map out a timeline that works with Mom's schedule too.

I'll send you a quick summary beforehand so you can glance it over.

Talk soon,
Tyler

Tyler,

5:30 is perfect—I'll call you from the car on the way home. One more thing: can you check if we've got the film from last Friday's Worthington game backed up? I want to review some clock management decisions before we meet. Feels like we left a few plays on the field.

See you Thursday.

Dad

Got it covered, Dad. The game film's all set and I've already queued up the key sequences. I also threw together a quick depth chart update for the new package—should make the drill structure cleaner.

Looking forward to Thursday.

Tyler

Tyler,

Just wanted to touch base about the family dinner your mom's been planning. She's thinking Sunday the 8th if that works for everyone. I know you've got a lot on your plate, but it'd be good to have you there. Between practice schedules and the shop, it'll be nice to get everybody around the table.

Can you give Megan a call and fill her in? She doesn't always catch my texts.

Let me know what you think.

Henry

Coach,

The 8th works great for me. I'll get ahold of Megan. Mom's already locked in the menu with me, so you know she's got everything covered.

One thing—Grandpa asked if he could come by for dinner. I told him I'd check with you first before I gave him a solid yes. What do you think?

See you Sunday.

Tyler

Hey Tyler,

Got a minute? I was trying to set up that email integration thing we talked about for the shop's appointment reminders. Your mom said you might have some time this weekend. The API key setup is giving me grief—keeps saying invalid credentials or something. I think I did what you said, but I'm probably missing something obvious.

Can you walk me through it again? I want to get this live before the summer rush starts.

Dad

Hey Dad,

Ha, yeah I got this. Mom mentioned you were pulling your hair out with it. Quick question—when it says "invalid credentials," is it on the initial OAuth flow or when you're trying to test the connection? That'll help me figure out if it's a key issue or a permissions problem.

I can jump on a call this weekend if that helps. Easier than typing through it. Either Saturday afternoon or Sunday morning works for me.

Let me know,
Tyler

Yeah, I think it's the OAuth thing you mentioned—first step, like. Gets through most of it then barks at me. Saturday afternoon is perfect, actually. Your mom's got that garden club thing, so she'll be out anyway.

One more thing—once we get this working, should I be worried about anything security-wise? Don't want some hacker grabbing our clients' emails.

Thanks for dealing with your old man's tech troubles.

Dad

Henry,

Got your last email about the calendar integration. Been a few days and I'm trying to sync practice schedules to my family's calendar—Karen's tired of me double-booking. The API key setup you sent over: I got it in, but the calendar's still not pulling the weekly practice times into Google Calendar like it should.

Quick question: is there a specific permission I'm missing, or do I need to redo the authentication? Happy to send you a screenshot of what I'm seeing if that helps.

Thanks for your help on this.

—Henry

Henry,

Most likely scenario: you've got the API key, but the calendar hasn't granted read/write permissions yet. Check your Google account's connected apps list—you might need to approve the request there.

Also, what calendar are you trying to pull from? Is it your school calendar or a personal one you're building? That'll matter for the auth flow.

Once you confirm that, I can walk you through the next step.

—Tyler

Tyler,

Checked the apps list—authorization looked good, so that wasn't it. The calendar I'm building is personal, but I'm trying to pull in events from the school athletic director's shared calendar. That might be the piece I'm missing.

Should I be requesting different permissions for a shared calendar, or is there a separate endpoint for that? I can wrestle with the docs, but figured you might have the quick answer.

Practice starts in an hour, so I'll be heads-down most of the day, but I can try whatever you recommend tonight.

—Henry

Henry,

That's the missing link. Shared calendars need calendar.readonly scope (which you probably have), but you also need the calendar ID from your athletic director's calendar—it's not a secret, but it's not obvious where to find it.

In Google Calendar, have him right-click the calendar name in the sidebar and select Settings. The calendar ID is at the bottom under "Integrate calendar"—looks like an email address. Grab that and plug it into your API request.

Should be smooth sailing after that. Let me know if you hit any walls.

—Tyler

Tyler,

We're trying to get the barbershop schedule system hooked up to Drive like we talked about last month. Been working on it between practices and chair cuts, but I hit a wall with the API permissions. Everything was syncing fine until I had to refresh the credentials yesterday, now it's just hanging.

Can you take a look when you get a chance? I've got the error logs in the shared folder under "Harmon's Heritage Cuts" > "Tech Issues." Nothing urgent—we're still writing everything down the old way—but I'd rather get this sorted before fall practice ramps up and I'm completely underwater.

Let me know what you need from me.

Coach

Dad,

Got your email. Pulled the logs—looks like the refresh token is expired and the scope wasn't set right on the new credentials. That's on me, I should've walked you through the setup better.

I'm pushing an updated service account file to the shared folder. All you have to do is download it, go to the API dashboard, and swap out the old credentials for the new ones. Takes about two minutes. Send me a message when you've done it and I'll test the sync from my end.

Talk soon.

Ty

Tyler,

Had a problem at the shop this morning—the iPad wasn't processing cards through Square. Lost about forty minutes before I switched to the old manual imprinter. Not ideal when you've got clients in the chair and a line out the door.

I checked the connection and it looked fine, but something's off with the Square app itself. Before I dig into it myself and waste more time, figured I'd loop you in. You've always been better with this tech stuff than I am.

Can you take a look when you get a chance? Let me know what you need from me—login info or whatever else helps you troubleshoot.

Thanks,
Dad

Hey Dad,

Got it. Sounds like the app may have crashed or the API key got corrupted. A few things to try:

1. Force quit the Square app and restart it
2. Check that your internet connection is solid (WiFi or ethernet)
3. Log out of Square completely and log back in

If none of that works, we might need to reinstall the app or generate a new API key. Let me know which step fixes it or if you're still stuck.

Also—how was the line? Sounds like a busy morning.

Tyler

Tyler,

Tried steps 1 and 2—nothing doing. Did the full logout and login and that did the trick. Processing cards fine now. Looks like the session just got corrupted somehow.

Appreciate you walking me through it instead of just telling me to call Square's support line. You know your old man would've spent an hour on hold.

One thing—could you set up something so this doesn't happen during game weeks? I've got enough on my plate without sweating the register. Just a thought.

Thanks again,
Dad
